fact: The Andrea Doria and Stockholm both had radar, among the first generation of shipboard radar intended to prevent collisions
source: Collision Course: The Classic Story of the Collision of the Andrea Doria and the Stockholm, Alvin Moscow (1959/2004)

fact: Dense fog off Nantucket, and both ships saw each other on radar but neither captain radioed the other to confirm intentions
source: Collision Course: The Classic Story of the Collision of the Andrea Doria and the Stockholm, Alvin Moscow (1959)

fact: At approximately eleven miles apart, both ships turned in the same direction, putting them on a direct collision course
source: Alive on the Andrea Doria! The Greatest Sea Rescue in History, Pierette Domenica Simpson (2006)

fact: The Stockholm's ice-strengthened bow penetrated approximately thirty feet into the Andrea Doria's starboard side
source: Collision Course, Alvin Moscow (1959). The Stockholm's reinforced bow for Baltic ice navigation penetrated deep into the Andrea Doria's hull.

fact: The ship listed so severely that the lifeboats on the port side could not be launched, leaving only half available
source: Out of the Fog: The Sinking of the Andrea Doria, Roger Vaughan (1996)

fact: Forty-six people died in the disaster despite the ship carrying enough lifeboat capacity for all passengers and crew
source: Widely documented; 46 deaths (5 Stockholm crew, 41 Andrea Doria passengers/crew) confirmed by official inquiries and multiple historical accounts
